summary: Joel recounts the saga of email search in Microsoft Outlook, which was so poor that a third-party tool called Lookout became essential. Microsoft acquired Lookout, removed it from the market, and then Outlook 2007 actively blocked it from running while offering an inferior built-in search powered by Windows Desktop Search. The built-in search was roughly 100 times slower than Lookout had been. A subsequent patch from Microsoft finally improved search performance enough to make Outlook 2007 usable, though still not as fast as the original Lookout.
key_points: Outlook's native search was so bad that users relied on third-party tools like Lookout, which used the open-source Lucene engine for sub-second full-text search
Microsoft acquired Lookout, removed it from the market, and Outlook 2007 specifically blocked it from running — while offering a far slower replacement
The Windows Desktop Search integration made searches take about 30 seconds instead of less than one second, a 100x regression
A later patch fixed the worst performance issues for users with large PST files, making Outlook 2007 finally adequate but still not as fast as the tool it killed
money_quotes: Search used to be horrible in Outlook. It was so bad that for all intents and purposes, you couldn't search your old email.
Microsoft did the only thing that made sense: they bought Lookout (the company) and took the product off the market.
When Outlook 2007 runs, it checks to see if Lookout was running and disabled it if it was.
The only possible explanation is that someone on the Outlook team is getting paid a bonus for convincing people to switch to Gmail.
Now I can search old email quickly enough that I don't forget what I was searching for by the time the results come up.
key_insight: When a platform owner acquires and kills a superior third-party tool, they take on an obligation to match its quality — and Microsoft's failure to do so with Outlook search turned an upgrade into a downgrade.
